title: Chapter Two: Prophet
content: The water tasted like clay and regret.

Aria swallowed it anyway — third morning in the Mirror of the Sky, third cup of the same mineral-thick water that coated her tongue like a chalk promise. She sat cross-legged on the woven mat that served as her bed in the guest dwelling, a round hut of packed earth and living wood that smelled of dried lavender and something older, something subterranean. The walls sweated in the pre-dawn cold.
A guest. Right. A guest with a guard posted at the door who smiled like he meant it.

She pulled on her borrowed clothes — rough-spun linen the color of wet sand, too loose at the shoulders, too tight at the hips — and pushed through the hide flap into the grey morning. The settlement sprawled below her perch on the hillside: thirty-odd structures of timber and rammed earth, rooftop gardens dripping with condensation, smoke rising from communal cook-fires in thin white threads. Beyond it, the valley floor faded into morning fog, and above it all, the mountains stood like_closed teeth.
Pretty. Isolated. A terrarium with one door.

She found Prisca by the central well, struggling with a wooden bucket that had seen better centuries. The girl's dark hair was braided with small white flowers — mountain pennyroyal, Aria had learned, though no one here called it that. They called it *Iris's breath*, which was either poetic or deeply unsettling.
"You're up early," Prisca said, heaving the bucket onto the well's stone rim. Water slopped over her wrists.

"Couldn't sleep. The water here is —" Aria stopped herself. No. Not complain. Contribute. That was the calculation she'd made on night one, staring at the ceiling while the guard shuffled outside: if they needed her, they wouldn't dispose of her. If she was useful, she became an asset instead of a problem. "Actually, can I see your water supply? The whole system?"
Prisca blinked. "The whole — why?"
"Because this water tastes like a geology textbook and I want to know why."

The settlement's water came from a springhouse up the hill, channeled through hand-dug clay pipes into a central cistern, then distributed by bucket and gravity. It was, Aria had to admit, not bad engineering for people who'd apparently lost the concept of metallurgy. But it was missing something crucial.
"You don't filter it," she said, kneeling by the cistern. She dipped her fingers and brought them to her nose. Silt. Organic particulate. A faint sulfurous undertone. "At all."
"We let it settle," said Old Fen, the wiry woman who managed the water works. She said it the way people say things that have always been true.
"Settling removes suspended solids. It doesn't remove dissolved contaminants, bacteria, or — look, it's making people sick, isn't it? I've heard the coughing."

Fen's jaw tightened. The settlement didn't like admitting weakness. But the coughing had been audible for two nights now — a wet, rattling sound from the eastern dwellings that nobody seemed eager to discuss.

Aria spent the morning building a water filter. It wasn't complicated — she'd done it in a sim lab once during her undergrad, a class exercise on appropriate technology for resource-limited communities. Gravel at the bottom of a cracked clay pot she found stacked behind the tannery, coarse sand in the middle, charcoal from the cook-fires crushed and layered on top, fine sand at the crown. She punched a hole in the pot's base with a stone, fitted a hollow reed, and sealed it with pine pitch.
The first pour came out black. The second, brown. The fifth ran clear.

She gave it to the coughing family first — three generations in one cramped hut, the grandmother's eyes rheumy, the children listless. They drank without questioning it because Prisca told them to, and Prisca had that earnest face that made people trust things.
Within an hour, the grandmother said her throat felt different. By evening, the children were asking for more.
"What did you do?" Prisca asked, watching the filter drip with the reverence of someone witnessing a birth.

"Basic physical chemistry. Adsorption and mechanical filtration. The charcoal grabs organic compounds and some bacteria, the sand catches particulates, the gravel provides drainage. It's not — it's not magic." Aria wiped her hands on her linen pants. "It's literally dirt in a pot."
But word traveled faster than she did. By nightfall, three more families had requested "the Prophet's filter," and someone — she never found out who — had started calling her that.
*Prophet.*

She hated it with a heat that surprised her. She'd spent her career fighting the conflation of science and faith, the way people stuffed knowledge they didn't understand into the shape of revelation, and here she was, twenty centuries out of time, being canonized for a middle-school science project.
"You're upset," Prisca observed that night, sitting outside Aria's hut while the guard pretended not to listen.
"I'm not upset. I'm — okay, I'm upset. I made a dirt sandwich in a flowerpot and they think I'm speaking to God."

"They think you see things they can't. Isn't that the same thing your people did?" Prisca tilted her head. "Wore white coats instead of white robes, but still — you believed what the seers told you, yes? The ones with the numbers?"
Aria opened her mouth. Closed it. "That's a depressingly good point."
Three days later, she read the sky.

It started as a pressure change — a dull ache behind her sinuses, the same feeling she'd get before monsoon season in the Asian subcontinent. She'd spent three years studying atmospheric rivers and their collapse, and her body had learned the precursors the way a sailor learns the smell of a squall: the veiled sun, the greenish cast to the western horizon, the way the valley wind died and then reversed direction in the space of an hour.
"Rain's coming," she told the work crew digging new root cellars. "Heavy. Four hours, maybe five."
They laughed. The sky was cloudless. Old Fen said it hadn't rained in seventeen days.

Aria checked the trees. The oak leaves had flipped silver-side up. The swallows were flying low and fast, hawking insects pushed down by the descending air mass. She could smell it now — that ionized, ozone-edged metallic tang that meant water was already falling somewhere upwind, charging the air like a live wire.
"Cover your stores," she said. "I'm not asking."

They covered them. Partly because the Prophet had spoken. Partly because there was something in her voice — not certainty, exactly, but the particular urgency of someone who'd watched weather kill people and didn't intend to let it happen again.

The storm hit at four hours, seventeen minutes. Nearly four inches of rain in two hours. The uncovered fields would have flooded; the root cellars would have filled. As it was, the settlement's gutters and channels handled the runoff, and the cisterns filled to capacity for the first time in Aria's memory.
Afterward, standing in the petrichor-soaked evening while the community celebrated with fermented berry wine and bonfires, she caught Iris watching her from across the fire.

The priestess-leader wore a simple cream dress tonight, her dark hair unbound and spilling over her shoulders, a circlet of living flowers — not pennyroyal, but something night-blooming, white petals luminous in the firelight — resting on her brow. She looked like a painting of a goddess. She looked like a trap dressed in garden party.

She crossed the fire circle and sat beside Aria on the log bench, close enough that Aria could smell her — rosewater and green woodsmoke and something underneath, something clean and mineral, like the spring.
"You've made yourself valuable," Iris said. The fire painted her face in gold and shadow.
"I've made myself *useful*. There's a difference."

"Is there?" Iris tilted her head. A strand of dark hair fell across her cheek. She didn't move it. "Valuable things are kept. Useful things are consumed." A pause. "Which would you prefer to be, Aria Chen?"
The way she said her name — low, precise, as if tasting each syllable — made Aria's pulse jump in a way that had nothing to do with fear and everything to do with danger of a different kind.
"I'd prefer to be neither. I'd prefer to leave."

"Of course you would." Iris smiled, and it was warm, it was always warm, that smile, but the warmth sat on top of something else the way霜 sits on glass — beautiful, structural, and very, very cold. "But you won't. Because you've already seen the spring."
Aria's stomach dropped. "What?"

"The spring that feeds our water. You've visited it three times now. I know because I've had you followed — I won't insult you by pretending otherwise. You go there because something troubles you about it. Something you haven't shared." Iris turned to face her fully, firelight dancing in her dark eyes. "You read the clouds, Aria. You read the water, the soil, the patterns in things. But you won't read me. Why?"
"Because you're not a weather system. Weather doesn't lie on purpose."
Iris laughed — a genuine sound, startled out of her, and for a moment the mask slip-slid and Aria saw something underneath: loneliness, raw and startled at being witnessed. Then it was gone.

"Dead knowledge," Iris said softly. "You carry dead knowledge in your bones. Equations for rain, formulas for water, taxonomies of blight. But you're afraid of the living thing — the thing that can't be calculated."
"Your spring is contaminated," Aria said, because the best defense against seduction was a fact. "That's not a metaphor. It's chemistry."
Iris didn't flinch. She nodded slowly, as if confirming something she'd already known. "Yes. The delegation came to me this morning. The water level is dropping. They're frightened."
"Did you tell them the truth?"

"I told them the sky provides and the earth remembers." Iris's voice was steady, melodic, the voice of someone who'd practiced calm until it became structural. "I told them we would endure. I held their hands and looked into their faces and I gave them the only thing I have to give — the performance of certainty."
Aria stared at her. "That's — you're running a theocracy on *confidence*."

"Every government is. Yours just called it something different." Iris stood, smoothing her dress. The night-blooming flowers caught a breath of wind and trembled. "Come to the spring with me tomorrow. I want to see what you see."
"Why?"

"Because you look at the world and it answers you. I look at the world and it *obeys* me." She touched Aria's shoulder — light, deliberate, a finger's-width of contact that burned like a brand. "I want to know which of us is right."

She walked away into the dark, and Aria sat by the fire for a long time after, watching the flames consume the wood, feeling the ghost-pressure of that touch on her shoulder, and hating herself for wanting it to return.
The blight came on day five.

Aria found it by accident, walking the eastern fields with Prisca, who'd appointed herself Aria's unofficial guide and protector. The bean crop — a hardy purple variety she'd never seen before — looked healthy at a glance. But Aria had learned to glance twice.
She knelt and lifted a lower leaf. The underside was stippled with small grey-white spots, each one rimmed in a faint halo of yellowish tissue.
"Shit."
"What?" Prisca crouched beside her.

"Is this — has anyone noticed these marks?" Aria was already scanning the neighboring plants. There. And there. And there, three rows over, the lesions more advanced, the leaf tissue necrotic and curling.
"They're just marks. The beans always —"

"These aren't just marks. This is *Cercospora* — or something like it. Fungal. See the halos? That's the toxin spreading into healthy tissue." Aria stood up, already counting rows, estimating spread rate. "If this is what I think it is, you have maybe ten days before it jumps to the grain fields. Maybe less if it rains again."
Prisca's face went pale. "We lost the grain stores two winters ago. If this crop —"
"Show me the seed bank. Every variety you have. I need to know what's resistant."

They worked through the afternoon, Aria systematically examining each stored crop sample while Prisca translated local names into practical descriptions. By evening, she'd identified two grain varieties that showed no susceptibility, mapped the infection's probable spread pattern, and recommended immediate burning of the affected bean rows as a firebreak.

The village council argued. Burning crops felt like sacrilege. Aria stood in the center of their meeting hall — a round building hung with dried herbs and woven talismans — and said, with a calm she didn't feel, "You can mourn the beans now and eat grain next winter, or mourn both while you starve."
They burned the beans.
And the name *Prophet* spread through the settlement like the very blight she'd stopped — except this contagion was made of awe and gratitude, and it clung to her skin worse than the chalk water.
That night, after the fires had burned down and the smoke smelled of charred vegetation and loss, Aria took her spectrometer and crept to the spring.

The device was the one piece of her old life she'd kept — a handheld Raman spectrometer, battered, solar-charged, its calibration slightly off but functional. She'd hidden it in the lining of her borrowed coat because Iris's people viewed technology the way Aria viewed theology: with suspicion, aesthetic revulsion, and the unexamined certainty that it had ended the world once and would do it again.

The springhouse was a small stone structure, half overgrown with moss, fed by a fracture in the granite below. The water emerged clear and cold, pooling in a stone basin before channeling into the pipes. In the moonlight, it looked pure enough to cry into.
Aria knelt, activated the spectrometer, and held its sensor in the flowing water.
The readings came in fragments — the device was old and the signal was weak — but she knew enough to read the spectrum like a face. Calcium. Magnesium. Sulfates. All expected. All natural.
Then the heavy element signatures.
Lead — elevated but not alarming. Arsenic — trace, within tolerance.
And uranium.

Not uranium in the abstract, uranium the element. Uranium-238 and, more critically, uranium-235 — the fissile isotope, the one that didn't occur in these ratios in nature. The 235/238 ratio was wrong. Enriched. Not weapon-grade, not even reactor-grade, but the unmistakable fingerprint of industrial processing.
Aria's hands were shaking. She ran the scan again. Same result.

This wasn't natural contamination leaching from the granite. This was something man-made, something engineered, something buried — and it was leaking into the water supply of a settlement that didn't know what radiation was, didn't know what it did to bones and blood and the unfolding cells of children, couldn't even conceive of the invisible violence being done to them with every cup they drank.
She cupped water in her palm and stared at it — clear, cold, innocent. The way poison always looked.
Something was down there. Under the mountain. Under the valley. Something old and wrong and patient, bleeding its silence into the spring.
And Iris — beautiful, certain, terrifying Iris — either knew and said nothing, or didn't know and was leading her people into a slow invisible death.
Aria didn't know which was worse.

She sat by the spring until dawn, the spectrometer's dying screen casting blue light on her fingers, and she thought about traps. About golden cages. About the look in Iris's eyes when she'd said *I want to see what you see*.
The morning birds began their indifferent singing, and Aria Chen — climate physicist, reluctant prophet, prisoner in paradise — made herself a promise: she would find what was under this mountain.
Even if it cost her the cage she was starting, against all reason, to love.
